courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Superheroes in Comics and Film
courses.description:
(UGED2187 is double-coded with ENGE2140.)

What’s a hero? What’s a superhero? Why and when do we need superheroes? How to be one and who gets to be one? We will begin by tracing the origins of this enduring pop fiction, from the hero’s journey in antiquity to the outbreak of the Second World War. We will also explore the moral ambiguity and mixed values of some of the most memorable characters. Furthermore, we will examine revisionist approaches to the superhero genre that respond to the hopes and disillusionments of the contemporary era. Topics covered will include good and evil, power and identity, and justice and responsibility. You may also have the chance to design your own superheroes. It is hoped that by the end of the course, you will develop new ways of reading superhero narratives as uniquely modern myths.
courses.enrollment_requirement:

courses.learning_outcomes:
Having successfully completed this course students shall be able to:

* comprehend the developments and significance of the superhero genre
* understand elements of effective storytelling
* analyse form, style, and technique in the interpretation of different mediums
* investigate, discuss and write critically, reflectively, and analytically

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Children's Literature
courses.description:
(UGED2194 is double-coded with ENGE2360.)

This course will provide an outline of developments in children’s literature in England and parts of Europe through the study of some essential, central texts as well as recent books for children. The uses of fantasy and the educational aspects of books for children will be discussed, along with notions of childhood and the nature of children. Through close reading of set texts students will be able to engage in critical techniques applicable to most literature, for the best texts for children satisfy sensitive adult readers too.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGED2194 is double-coded with ENGE2360. 
Not for English; English Studies and English Language Education (ELED) Majors; and students who have taken ENGE2360. 
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. To analyse the pedagogical and cultural values of children’s literature.

2. To evaluate the techniques employed in writing children’s literature and in representations of children.

3. To explore the social, historical and political background of the themes and motifs in children’s literature.

4. To critically evaluate the ideas and values embedded in children’s literature.
courses.course_syllabus:
Introduction: “Childhood is the Kingdom where Nobody Dies” - We will look at childhood as an inner affective condition rather than a biological condition.

Approaches to Reading Fairy Tales: Grimms’ Fairy Tales / Andersen‘s Fairy Tales – We will explore themes such as good vs evil and gender representation in fairy tales.

Romantic Notions of Childhood: Songs of Innocence and Experience – We will analyse how the poems convey social critique.

Representations of Childhood in Victorian Literature: [Extracts provided] – We will evaluate Victorian notions of class and how this influences the representations of children in literature.

A Child’s Journey: Alice’s Adventures in Wonderland / Peter Pan – We will analyse elements of fantasy in children’s literature.

Children as Heroic Figures: Harry Potter and the Philosopher’s Stone – We will analyse how children are represented as heroes in children’s literature.

Illusion vs Reality: Harry Potter and the Curious Incident – We will explore how modern anxieties are expressed in children’s literature.

Narrating the Real World: The Curious Incident of the Dog in the Night-time – We analyse the genre of realism in children’s literature.

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Gods Behaving Badly: Myths and Legends from Around the World
courses.description:
(UGED2197 is double-coded with ENGE2190.)

The course will consider myths and legends from around the world with particular sensitivity to one peculiar aspect of them: that the gods depicted in them often behave no better than the people whose lives they determine. In order to explain why this is the case, the course will seek to understand the nature and function of myth, and the complex uses to which they are put in order to explain some of our most pressing questions: How did we all begin? Where are we? Who are we? Why is there so much wrong with the world? What’s the solution? Where will we go when we die? How will it all end? Although the concept of myth might seem outdated in modern secular culture, by drawing comparisons between the ancient and contemporary worlds, students will be encouraged to see how myth-making of some kind is as inevitable and innately human as the questions which prompt it. The course will begin by establishing some archetypal similarities between myths – from myths of Creation to myths of Apocalypse – and then consider myths from specific cultures – including Greco-Roman, Scandinavian, and Chinese – before considering the appeal of myth in contemporary popular culture, especially The Lord of Rings, and Game of Thrones.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGED2197 is double-coded with ENGE2190. 
Not for English; English Studies and English Language Education (ELED) Majors; and students who have taken ENGE2190. 
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.learning_outcomes:
* Students will acquire knowledge of the different types of myths from ancient and contemporary worlds in order to comprehend the similarities and differences between them.

* Students will learn to analyse various myths as vehicles for cultural values and anxieties.

* Students will evaluate the power and persuasiveness of myths to give answers to presiding moral and existential questions including the meaning of life, the nature of death, and the existence of God.

* Students will synthesise their knowledge of a broad range of myths in order to understand the greater role of myth in human society.

* Students will critique the way contemporary technology has facilitated a new conception of mythology and myth-making.

* Students will evaluate the representation of gender in myth.
courses.course_syllabus:
| Week | Lecture | Topics and Reading\* |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Introduction | Definition of “myth”: *muthos* Archetypes across cultures |
| 2 | What is a God? | Gods as archetype, the Great Mother Gender roles and the gods Demi-gods and Godlike Humans |
| 3 | How did it all begin?: Creation Myths | Creation stories from Australian Aboriginal, Babylonian, Christian, Norse, and Japanese narratives. Reading: *Genesis* (selections); “Enuma Elish” (selections); Hesiod, *Theogony* (selections) |
| 4 | Why do I suffer? Myths about Evil | The Problem of Evil Christian and Islamic accounts of the birth of evil Early Babylonian Poetry Reading: *Genesis* (selections); *Quran* (selections) |
| 5 | How will it all end?: Myths of the Apocalypse | Disaster Myths The Great Flood Reading: *Revelation* (selections); “Atrahasis” (selections). |
| 6 | Where do we go?: Myths of the Afterlife | Heaven/Elysium/Valhalla and Hell/Hades/Tartarus Final Judgement Reading: Homer, Odyssey (selections from book 11); Virgil, *Aeneid* (selections from book 6); Dante, Inferno (selections from canto 1). |
| 7 | From Zeus to Jupiter: Greco-Roman Myth | The Titans and Olympians The Pantheon of Gods Heroes and Gods Reading: Ovid, *Metamorphoses* (selections from book 1) |
| 8 | Scandinavian Legend: The Sagas | Justice and Revenge Ancestry Reading: *The Sagas* (selections) |
| 9 | Irish Myths | Dierdre of the Sorrows Yeats and the Revival of Myth Myths of the Landscape |
| 10 | Chinese Myths and Folklore | Mother Goddesses Demons Stories about the Stars and Moon |
| 11 | Old Gods and the New: *GOT*, *LOTR*, and Modern Myth | Contemporary uses of old archetypes Reading: *The Lord of the Rings* (selections); “On Fairy Tales” (selections) |
| 12 | The Gods of a Godless World: Technology and Social Media | Technology as God Social media as myth-telling |
| 13 | Concluding lecture | |

* *\*Reading will typically be 10- 15 pages a week.**

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Philosophy and Human Life
courses.description: This course investigates problems in different realms of human life from a philosophical point of view. It also demonstrates how philosophical thinking can make a distinctive contribution to our understanding of ourselves, others, and the world. Topics to be discussed include: different systems of value, human happiness, religion and ultimate concern, and ethical issues in modern life.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for Philosophy Majors.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Discuss ethical issues concerning human life.
2. Critically analyze different ideas of happiness in modern society.
3. Reflect on the meaning of life in philosophical way.
4. Evaluate and reflect on one’s identity.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *(1-5) 核心內容。任教老師必須講授的專題**

1. 導論：哲學與人生的關係

2. 人生哲學原理

3. 幸福問題

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Introduction to Asian Art: The Art and Archaeology of West, South, Southeast, and Inner Asia
courses.description:
(UGED2315 is double-coded with FAAS2113.)

The survey introduces the art and material culture of pre-20th century Asia with particular attention paid to trans-regional and cross-cultural interactions. Select artistic traditions in West (mainly, Iran), South (India, Sri Lanka, and the Himalayas), Southeast (Indonesia, Burma, Thai, Cambodia, Laos, and Vietnam), and Inner (Afghanistan, Tajikistan, Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan, Kyrgyzstan, Turkmenistan, and Mongolia) will be examined against their philosophical, religious, socio-political, economic, aesthetic, and technological backgrounds. Major themes include (1) the rise of civilizations, (2) developments of various artistic forms and media, (3) tombs and funerary paraphernalia, (4) the spread of Buddhism, Hinduism, Islamism, and other worldviews across the region, and (5) Indianization and Sinicization.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGED2315 is double-coded with FAAS2113.
Not for Fine Arts Majors; and students who have taken FAAS2113.
Co-requisite: UGFH1000, UGFN1000 or UGFN1001 (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.course_syllabus:

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Human Happiness
courses.description: This course is a philosophical discussion of the idea of happiness. The first part covers major theories of happiness from Western and Chinese tradition. The second part deals with the theoretical issues of happiness: the definition of happiness; distinction between pleasure, happiness, and unhappiness; attainability of happiness; hedonism and eudaemonism; and the meaning of happiness in the contemporary world.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for Philosophy Majors.
Co-requisite: UGFH1000, UGFN1000 or UGFN1001 (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Identify the many facets of happiness and the complexity of the issues involved.

2. Critically analyze and evaluate how the idea of happiness has been shaped in the modern world.

3. Conceptualize various philosophical thoughts of human happiness throughout history.

4. Reflect upon and analyze ethical, psychological, and anthropological questions concerning human happiness;

5. Elaborate on the complexity and subjectivity of the issues of human happiness.

6. Cultivate intellectual interests in thinking about the relationship between happiness and human value.

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Understanding Comic Arts
courses.description:
(UGED2326 is double-coded with CURE2051.)

Nowadays comic strip has become one of the major forms of visual narratives in our culture. Both comics and film are visual arts originally developed within the period of late 19th century and early 20th century. Although film has long ago established its artistic legitimacy, comic art still struggle for recognition. However, as Will Eisner put it, comic strip is a “Sequential Art” and has its own unique aesthetic nature. This course tries to inquire the governing aesthetic principles of comics and investigate its unique method of expression. We will also try to delineate the special communication mode of comics and its relationship with different aspects of modern society and culture. Students will learn to appreciate comic art from different perspectives, and strengthen analytical skills for visual media.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGED2326 is double-coded with CURE2051. 
Not for Cultural Studies; Public Humanities; Religious Studies Majors; and students who have taken CURE2051. 
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Through the lectures, students will recognize the aesthetic nature of sequential art and locate the humanistic values and meanings of comic arts for modern society and culture.

2. Through tutorial discussions, student will apply the analytical skills and formal methods to interpret comic and visual texts.

3. Through mid-term assignment (research portfolio on the visual representation of comics), student will distinguish, compare, analyze and criticize different visual and multimedia sources.

4. Through final paper, student will prepare and manage to do an academic research on visual representation.

5. Student will aware, judge and appraise the practical values of the analytical skills and historical knowledge provided by this course after they complete the mid-term assignment and final paper.

* *Section 3: Reception, Fan Culture and Cultural Economy of Comic Art**

8. Narrator and Reader in Comic Narrative

·蕭湘文〈漫畫中的意識型態〉。《漫畫研究──傳播觀點的檢視》。台北市：五南圖書出版股份有限公司，2002。

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: "The Book of Changes" and Wisdom of Life
courses.description: "The Book of Changes" (Yijing) is a highly venerated and influential classic that has made far-reaching impacts on the Chinese religion, philosophy, politics and medicine. It has a far greater significance for the Chinese culture as a book of wisdom than a divination manual, by offering a wealth of knowledge and insights about the cosmos, nature, and life. It shows us the ways to cultivate virtue, enhance ability and perceive wisdom by living our lives and making judgments in harmony with the changing forces of the universe. The store of ancient wisdom accumulated in this classic is often still relevant to our contemporary world that is in constant and rapid technological, social and political changes. Through guided reading of the key notions and themes encapsulated in some selected hexagrams, students may draw from the wellspring of profound wisdom in the "The Book of Changes" to stimulate their reflection on the approach of life, the way of looking at the world, and the recurring issues facing humanity.

courses.course_syllabus:
1. The Making of the “Classic of classics” (「群經之首」的成書)
2. The Interpretations of *The Book of Changes* (易經的詮釋角度)
3. *The Book of Changes* as the “Book of wisdom” (易經為「智慧之書」)
4. Qian Hexagram and Creative Force (乾卦與自強不息)
5. Kun Hexagram and Magnanimity (坤卦與寬厚包容)
6. Meng Hexagram and Enlightenment (蒙卦與啟蒙教化)
7. Tai Hexagram and Great Peace (泰卦與和諧通泰)
8. Qian Hexagram and Modesty (謙卦與謙虛處下)
9. Fu Hexagram and Rejuvenation (復卦與回復正氣)
10. Yi Hexagram and Self-nourishment (頤卦與自力更生)
11. Kan Hexagram and Perseverance (坎卦與堅忍不拔)
12. Gen Hexagram and Tranquility (艮卦與寧靜致遠)
13. Jing Hexagram and Living Water (井卦與活水泉源)

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Being Human in the Age of AI: An Introduction to Digital Ethics
courses.description:
(UGED2329 is double-coded with CURE2008.)
Artificial intelligence (AI) and data science are reshaping every aspect of contemporary life—from how we work and communicate to how decisions are made about health, education, and governance. This course introduces the ethical, social, and philosophical issues that arise from these technologies. Students will examine topics such as algorithmic bias, data surveillance, automation, misinformation, and AI’s impact on human agency and creativity. Drawing on philosophical frameworks, scientific research, and real-world case studies, the course encourages students to think critically about how technological systems embody values and power structures, and how they can be governed responsibly. The course aims to develop students’ ethical reasoning, cross-disciplinary understanding, and ability to articulate informed positions on emerging technologies. Through a team-based learning approach, students engage in weekly readings, discussions, and collaborative writing exercises that cultivate both independent judgment and cooperative problem-solving. No technical background is required.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGED2329 is double-coded with CURE2008.
Not for Cultural Studies; Public Humanities; Religious Studies Majors; and students who have taken CURE2008.
Co-requisite: Any course with "UGFH" or "UGFN" as prefix
courses.learning_outcomes:
* *Students should be able to:**

1. Analyze and Apply Ethical Frameworks: Identify and apply key ethical theories (e.g., virtue ethics, deontology, utilitarianism) to evaluate the design and deployment of AI and data-driven technologies.
2. Evaluate Global Societal Impacts: Critically assess the social and political implications of algorithmic systems—such as bias, surveillance, and data colonialism—integrating diverse global and cross-cultural perspectives.
3. Construct Ethical Arguments: Formulate and defend informed, persuasive positions on the responsible governance and ethical use of emerging technologies in contemporary society.
4. Collaborate and Communicate Effectively: Work productively in teams to synthesize complex ethical ideas and communicate them clearly through collaborative writing and oral presentations.
5. Practice Critical Reflexivity: Reflect on one’s own assumptions, values, and agency as a user, designer, or citizen within an AI-mediated world.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Week 1: Introduction – Value and Technology**
How do technology and human values intersect? Explore Heidegger’s and Bryson’s perspectives on technology as a cultural practice, and discuss technology’s role in shaping human existence and meaning.

* *Week 2: Foundations of AI and Data Science**
What are artificial intelligence, machine learning, and data science? Examine foundational concepts, basic algorithms, and the societal narratives that frame technological innovation.

* *Week 3: Ethical Frameworks – Virtue, Duty, and Utility**
How can ethics guide technology? Introduce virtue ethics and human flourishing, Kantian deontology, and utilitarian reasoning as frameworks for evaluating technological decisions.

* *Week 4: Data and Surveillance Capitalism**
How does data become a source of power? Investigate the political economy of data extraction, corporate surveillance, and the ethics of privacy, consent, and digital autonomy.

* *Week 5: Algorithmic Bias and Fairness**
How do biases enter algorithms? Analyze social and technical dimensions of algorithmic bias, justice, accountability, and transparency through real-world case studies.

* *Week 6: AI as a Mirror of Humanity**
In what ways does AI reflect us? Explore how machine learning systems reproduce human cognition, prejudice, and creativity, and question the limits of “artificial” intelligence.

* *Week 7: Data Colonialism and Global Inequality**
How does AI reproduce global power asymmetries? Study postcolonial and Global South critiques of data extraction, digital labor, and the ethics of global governance.

* *Week 8: Responsibility and the “Moral Gap”**
Who is accountable for AI’s actions? Examine the “responsibility gap” in autonomous systems and discuss moral agency, liability, and ethical design.

* *Week 9: Online Manipulation and Persuasive Design**
How do digital platforms shape behavior? Investigate the behavioral economics of attention, nudging, dark patterns, and the ethics of algorithmic persuasion in social media.

* *Week 10: Deepfakes, Truth, and Misinformation**
What happens when truth becomes malleable? Discuss the epistemology of misinformation, authenticity in AI-generated media, and implications for democracy and public trust.

* *Week 11: Gamification and the Algorithmic Self**
How does gamification transform communication and learning? Analyze the role of AI in shaping motivation, reward systems, and social interaction in digital environments.

* *Week 12: In-Class Essay Examination**
Students compose a critical essay applying one or more ethical frameworks to a contemporary issue in AI and data ethics. Emphasis on synthesis, argumentation, and reflection.

* *Week 13: Conclusion – Ethics and the Future of AI**
What does a responsible AI future look like? Recap key themes, revisit philosophical questions, and reflect on individual and collective responsibility in an AI-mediated society.

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Reading Chinese Painting
courses.description:
(UGED2338 is double-coded with FAAS2101.)
Symbols and images that carry traces of Chinese cultural traditions can be found everywhere in the modern world. This course provides a comprehensive overview of the history of Chinese painting and trains students’ abilities in reading images so that they can achieve a deeper understanding of the cultural meanings embedded in everyday encounters. Through a broad survey of Chinese painting from the pre-Song period to the present, students will become familiar with major works, figures, theories, and movements, while also developing fundamental art-historical skills for observing and analyzing images’ style, subject matter, and socio-cultural meaning. In-class discussion of readings and writing exercises will teach students how to read scholarly literature and translate their observations and research on paintings into various forms of writing, such as academic reports, museum labels, or catalogue entries.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGED2338 is double-coded with FAAS2101.
Not for Fine Arts Majors; and students who have taken FAAS2101.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction to the course: Ice breaker

This introductory session will guide students through reading selected entries from Maxwell K. Hearn, *How to Read Chinese* *Paintings* (The Metropolitan Museum of Art and Yale University Press, 2008), to help them understand the basic formats and relationship between imagery and text in Chinese painting.

2. Figural painting (I): Line

This session will introduce major figural paintings from the pre-Song period and examine the historical, cultural, and literary contexts in which they emerged. It also explores the significance of “line” as a fundamental expressive element in Chinese painting.

3. Figural painting (II): Narrative

This session will investigate major figural paintings from the Song period onward and analyze the historical and cultural factors that led to the gradual decline of figural painting as a dominant genre in later periods.

4. Landscape painting (I): Style

This session will analyze the stylistic characteristics of landscape paintings from northern and southern China prior to the Southern Song period and examine the political and philosophical contexts that contributed to the emergence of this new genre as a representative form of Chinese painting.

5. Landscape painting (II): Text and image

This session will explore the relationship between text/poem and image in paintings from the Southern Song period onward, as well as the related yet distinct aesthetic ideals associated with courtly, Buddhist, and literati contexts.

6. Landscape painting (III): Painting schools

This session will examine the specific temporal and spatial conditions, political environment, and social structures that contributed to the emergence of Chinese “painting schools” in the Ming and Qing periods.

7. Paintings genres and art collecting

This session will examine the importance of art collecting within the Chinese political tradition and survey a range of lesser-known genres of Chinese painting, considering their changing significance within major art collections from the Song period to the present.

8. Bird-and-flower painting: Ink and color

This session will examine major bird-and-flower paintings, analyzing their artistic techniques as well as their cultural, political, and literary significance across historical periods.

9. Religious painting (I): Iconography

This session will introduce the transmission, visual features, and theological aspects of Chinese Buddhist and Daoist painting, with particular emphasis on the methods and significance of iconographical research.

10. Religious painting (II): Painting workshop

This session will explore the workshop practices involved in the creation of Chinese paintings, as well as the political and economic contexts in which they emerged.

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Chinese Literature in Modern Media
courses.description: This course aims to explore the creative transformation of the Chinese literary tradition in modern media. It seeks to analyze the aesthetic and cultural changes that occur when Chinese literary works are adapted into visual forms. We will compare the similarities and differences between original Chinese literary texts and their comics, animation, film and television adaptations in terms of content, form and artistic techniques. Through a multi-perspective evaluation of works across different media, we will also examine the cultural implications embedded within these adaptations. The course uses classic examples from Chinese literature as a foundation to systematically explore the theories and practices of cross-media adaptations of Chinese literary works. The objective is to cultivate students' ability to appreciate, analyze, and critically evaluate Chinese literature and culture.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for Chinese Studies Majors; and Contemporary China Studies Majors.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.learning_outcomes:
After studying in this course, a student will be able to:

1. Explain the nature, key characteristics, and differences of the two media: text and image.

2. Elucidate major theories and approaches to adapting literary works into film and television, thereby cultivating students’ cross-media capacities for appreciation and critical review.

3. Compare Chinese literary source texts with their multimedia adaptations in terms of content, form, and expressive techniques; analyze the cultural implications involved; and develop multi-perspectival evaluations of works across different media.
courses.course_syllabus:
第一部份：導論：文學的影視改編與跨媒介敘事

Part I: Introduction: Literary Adaptations and Trans-Media Narratives

講題一：文學與影像的對話

Topic 1: Dialogue Between Literature and Visual Media

講題二：二十世紀中國：文學與電影的轉換

Topic 2: 20th-Century China: Transformations Between Literature and Film

講題三：理解跨媒介敘事：以水墨動畫為例

Topic 3: Understanding Trans-Media Narratives: The Case of Ink-Wash Animation

第二部份：現代媒體上的文化再現與文化記憶

Part II: Cultural Representation and Cultural Memory in Modern Media

講題四：多媒體改編中的角色造型設計與文化記憶

Topic 4: Character Design and Cultural Memory in Multimedia Adaptations

講題五：民間傳說改編中的自由戀愛與性別意識

Topic 5: Free Love and Gender Consciousness in the Adaptations of Folk Legends

講題六：歷史人物的現代轉譯

Topic 6: The Modern Translation of Historical Figures

講題七：「香港—上海」雙城：現代小說改編中的跨地文化

Topic 7: “Hong Kong–Shanghai,” A Tale of Two Cities: Translocal Culture in the Adaptations of Modern Fiction

講題八：武俠新詮：武俠小說的電影與電玩改編

Topic 8: New Interpretations of Martial Arts: Film and Video Game Adaptations of Martial Arts Novels

講題九：香港文學改編中的都市懷舊文化與民族國家寓言

Topic 9: Urban Nostalgia and National Allegories in the Adaptations of Hong Kong Literature

第三部份：多媒體改編與跨媒介敘事中的形式實驗

Part III: Formal Experiments in Multimedia Adaptation and Trans-Media Narratives

講題十：詩歌與圖像的敘事表達

Topic 10: Narrative Expression Between Poetry and Image

講題十一：古典小說敘事與華語電影詩學

Topic 11: Classical Fictional Narrative and the Poetics of Chinese-Language Cinema

講題十二：跨媒介故事世界的構築之道

Topic 12: Ways of Building Trans-Media Storyworlds

講題十三：紀錄片與文學史敘述模式的轉變

Topic 13: Documentary and the Shifts in the Narrative Modes of Literary History

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Understanding Movies
courses.description:
(UGED2637 is double-coded with COMM2930.)

This course aims at helping students to earn a solid vocabulary and a sharp eye in analyzing films. Students will learn about the basic cinematic techniques and structures, including mise-en-scene and montage, use of cinematic time and space, the image, soundtrack, and the script. Theoretical perspectives such as realism, feminism, postmodernism would be introduced to address the political, economic and socio-cultural implications of movies. Equipped with these analytical tools, students can critically appreciate movies of different era and different places.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGED2637 is double-coded with COMM2930. 
Not for GCOM; COMM Majors; and students who have taken COMM2930. 
Co-requisite: UGFH1000, UGFN1000 or UGFN1001 (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students will be able to develop cine-literacy and analyze movies through the comprehension of film languages.

2. Students will be able to relay the movies to their broader contexts of production and consumption and through which to understand the culturaldiversity.

3. Students will be able to critically reflect upon ideologies underlying the film texts.

4. Students will be able to discuss existing and emergent challenges facing by human world through the discussion of the relationship of movies and current thoughts such as feminism and post-modernism.

5. Students will be able to produce a critical film analysis that reflects upon political, social and cultural issues embedded in film texts.
courses.course_syllabus:
The course consists of three main parts: (I) film form and aesthetics (week 1 to 4); (II) two main categories of movies (week 5 and 6); (III) critical theories (week 7 to 12). The first part equips students with specific film languages for film appreciation. The discussion of avant-garde movies and genre draws students’ attention to the dynamics as well as the competition between the two major modes of cultural productions. The last part introduces a variety of critical theories that will lead the understanding of movies to the rethinking of significant issues of human concerns. Key concepts will be introduced so that students can apply them in formulating their film analysis. The sequence of these issues implies a progression of knowledge from film specific to interdisciplinary approach and from film appreciation to critical evaluation.

The following is an outline of the course:

(1) Introduction: Film Styles: Realism and Formalism

This lecture will introduce the two major styles of films: realism and formalism. It aims to explain how film is being perceived as an art form and as a representation of reality. The introduction leads students to think of the nature of movie.

Key concept: film style, formalism and realism

Movies: *The Rules of the Game* (1939), *Last Year at Marienbad* (France/Italy 1961),《香港製造》(1997), 《天水圍的日與夜》(2008), 《東邪西毒》(1994),

(2) Film Narrative

This lecture will talk about how story is being narrated in movies through the elaboration of film structure. The concept of motif is highlighted to direct students to pay attention to how visual elements are contributed to the story telling in movies.

Key words: narrative Structure, story and plot, motifs,

Movies: *Rashomon* (1950), *Confessions* (告白，2011)

(3) Film Style: mise-en-scene

This lecture will present a major concept used in film appreciation—mise-en- scene. It explains how various visual elements including set design, costume, make-up, framing, cinematography, lighting etc. are articulating the meaning of the movies.

Key word: mise-en-scene

Movies: *The Conformist* (1970), *Clockwork Orange* (1971), *Old Boy* (2003), *the Artist* (2002)

(4) Film Style: Editing and Sound

This lecture will elucidate how post-production techniques such as editing, sound mixing and music can create meaning. The concepts will be related to the two major film styles of realism and formalism.

Key words: montage, continuity

Movies:*t**he Battleship Potemkin* (1925), *Psycho* (1960), *Godfather* (1972), *Apocalypse Now* (1979), *Run Rola Run* (1998)

(5) Auteur Theory and Avant-garde Movies

After introducing basic film languages in the previous lessons, this lecture will introduce auteur theory and avant-garde movies to exemplify movie as art. Auteur theory stresses the importance of understanding movies as imprints of film directors’ aesthetic policy and creative deployment of film languages. The discussion will lead to examination of the nature of avant-garde movie that embraces experimental spirit.

Keywords: auteur theory, avant-garde

Movies: *Meshes of the Afternoon* (1943), *Breathless* (1960), *Kika* (1993), *Volver* (2007)

(6) Film genres and blockbusters

This lecture introduces the opposite of avant-garde movies – genre movies. It also relates conventional making with Hollywood movies production

Keywords: genres, spectatorship.

Movies: *Chicago (2003)*, *Perhaps Love* (2005), *Sin City* (2005)

(7) Movies: Film and Ideologies: Class and Ethnic

This lecture aims to go beyond film languages by relate films to broader social contexts. The concept of ideology will be introduced to allow students to critically examine the power relations underpinning film texts. Class and racial stereotypes in movies will be used to demonstrate the ideological contestation and identity politics in movies.

Key Words: Ideology, stereotype

Movies: *Color Purple* (1985), *Titanic* (1997),

(8) Movies: Gender and Sexuality

This lecture continues the discussion of identity politics by analyzing how gender and sexuality are being represented and constructed in movies. Feminism and queer theory will be introduced not only to address the subject matters but also explain the transformation of film critiques from ideological approach to broader cultural studies of institutions and audiences.

Key concepts: feminism, gender, sexuality, queer theory, camp

Movies: 《金枝玉葉》(1994), *Boys Don’t Cry* (1999)

(9) Movies and the Nation

This lecture will introduce how the idea of national cinema is expressed and transformed. It problematizes the nation-state, sensitizing students’ awareness of the nation as constructed imagined communities of which the cinema takes a central place.

Keywords: nation-state, imagined communities

Movies: *The Birth of a Nation (*1915),《賽德克·巴萊》(2011)

(10) Movies and Globalization:

While the previous lecture delineates the significance of movies in building national identity, this lecture will discuss the consequence of the growing trend of globalization of movies production. Issues such as cultural homogenization and heterogenization, the clash of civilizations and local appropriation would be addressed.

Keywords: globalization, cultural homogenization and heterogenization, crash of civilization

Movies: *Infernal Affairs* (2003), *the Departed* (2006), *Avatar* (2005), the *Innocence of Muslims* (2012)

(11) Movies and Post-modernism

This lecture will address how movies are responded, aesthetically and thematically, to issues brought about by the distrust of modernism and the over-development of modernity. The discussion will address particularly the crisis of self-identity.

Key words: post-modernism, deconstruction, self-identity

Movies: *Metropolis* (1927), *the Matrix* (1999), *Suzhou River* (2000)

(12) Synthesis: Citizen Kane

This lecture uses the classical movie “Citizen Kane” to illustrate the complex nature of movie and the synthesis of multiple language systems in film texts.

Movies: *Citizen Kane* (1941)

(13) Group Presentation

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Ethics and the Human Experience
courses.description:
(UGED2666 is double-coded with ANTH2360.)

This course introduces students to the study of morality and ethics from a social and cross-cultural perspective. Morality and ethics became a distinct area for anthropological inquiry as recently as the beginning of the 21st century. Why this topic is relatively new in anthropology has something to do with the way in which moral differences have been conflated with cultural differences in the history of the discipline. In other words, anthropologists have long presumed that differences between right and wrong, good and bad, are culturally constructed. In recent years, however, this conflation has been questioned and problematized, with reference to the problem of competing moral goods within a single society and the role of the individual in negotiating moral commitments. Having much to say about cultural differences as well as contingent social processes, anthropologists are now in an even better position to contribute to cross-disciplinary conversations about the nature of morality and ethics. By exploring morality and ethics from a social and cross-cultural perspective, this course will demonstrate that morality and ethics is by no means an abstract subject best studied by philosophers. Students will be encouraged to see ethics as a part of their everyday lives, and to see the lives of others as shaped by shared human problems such as how to get along with other people, and how to get along with oneself.

This course is divided into four major units. It begins with an issue highly relevant to university students: the responsibility individuals carry for their own success and self-fulfillment in capitalist societies. The second unit considers why moral and cultural differences have been conflated, and how different societies have responded to shared human problems in different ways. The third unit explores the relationship between morality and the creation and maintenance of social relationships. Case studies of small communities in different parts of the world will be especially helpful for understanding morality and ethics from a social perspective, as cooperation can be fragile yet so important in small communities. Topics and readings in the last unit offer resources for enriching and expanding our vocabulary of virtues. Students will be invited to conduct research into the mundane things ordinary people do to take care of one another in daily life.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGED2666 is double-coded with ANTH2360.
Not for Anthropology Majors; and students who have taken ANTH2360.
Co-requisite: UGFH1000, UGFN1000 or UGFN1001 (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students will acquire intellectual resources for thinking about ethics and morality from a holistic perspective.

2. Students will learn to differentiate between the various factors that create unique social, cultural and historical circumstances.

3. Students will learn to connect the seemingly mundane to broader philosophical themes.

4. Students will have a better understanding of their own life experience.

5. Students will apply the skills they have learned in doing a class presentation on a topic of their choosing, in collaboration with group-mates.

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Preserving Cultural Heritage
courses.description:
(UGED2668 is double-coded with ANTH2730.)

This course aims to give students the basic knowledge and training on cultural heritage preservation, including archaeological excavation, report and analysis, and the preservation of ancient architectures and monuments. The course includes the following topics:
(1) concepts and international conventions/policies of cultural heritage preservation; (2) archaeological recording and analysis; and
(3) preservation of ancient architectures and monuments: intangible and digital heritage standards and procedures.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGED2668 is double-coded with ANTH2730. 
Not for Anthropology Majors; and students who have taken ANTH2730. 
Co-requisite: UGFH1000, UGFN1000 or UGFN1001 (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.learning_outcomes:
After taking this course, students are expected to:

1) understand the basic concepts, principles and approaches of managing cultural heritage.

2) learn to appreciate cultural heritage created by human beings, and to respect different cultures.

3) understand the economic, social and political issues of heritage preservation and management in contemporary societies.

4) cultivate the ability of inter-disciplinary thinking and critical thinking.

5) broaden the academic horizons and knowledge.
courses.course_syllabus:
Shall we preserve the old things, and why? What is “cultural heritage”? Using examples in different parts of the world, this course aims to give students a concise and critical understanding on heritage conservation from an anthropological perspective. The course will introduce basic concepts and cultural contexts of the occurrence and development of cultural heritage conservation in the world, and discuss important issues in this field, including the economic, social and political meanings of heritage in contemporary societies, the impetus of preserving heritage, and the use of heritage as a social and political implement by the state, scholars, and different communities for their own interests. The course will also discuss problems and challenges in this area.

No prior knowledge on heritage preservation is required for this course.

Major contents of this course are as follows:

Why are human beings interested in their past? Is heritage “invented”?

The origin and development of heritage conservation will be explored and discussed.

Debates on heritage preservation and management:

1. Who needs cultural heritage? Is heritage conservation a social elite business?

2. Who owns the cultural heritage, and who has the right to decide what should be reserved, and how to preserve it?

3. International principles and local cultures, globalization and localization: the preservation of cultural heritage in different political, economic and social contexts/ countries—should there be “universal principles and philosophy” for heritage conservation? Is there a so-called “best practice”? Who has the right to decide what is “the best practice”?

Course Evaluation Questionnaire

Emails and discussions will be used as feedback strategies during the course to collect feedback from students.

courses.description: Different values and beliefs, opposing clinical objectives, and resource limitations are persistent issues in medical and healthcare settings, from which ethical dilemmas could arise among healthcare practitioners, patients, and their family members, who are unavoidably engaged in the decision-making process in difficult health, life, and death scenarios. These scenarios may include but are not limited to organ donation, resuscitation of a terminally ill loved one, withdrawing or withholding life-sustaining and standard curative treatments for an end-of-life patient, active euthanasia and physician-assisted suicide, as well as healthcare rationing. While a well-established legal and health system can provide guidelines for walking through these processes, the values and beliefs of the patients, healthcare workers, and other decision-makers (including family members and guardians) would shape the way decisions are made. Therefore, understanding the factors that may shape or influence one’s values and moral beliefs is crucial for any meaningful discussions to resolve these dilemmas. This course will use a systematic approach to guide students in understanding the moral theories and principles pertinent to the works of bioethics, identifying the morally salient features and arguments of selected clinical, healthcare, and health-related scenarios, discussing and analyzing the main bioethical issues of those scenarios, as well as describing ways to resolve the relevant moral dilemma. Students will be introduced to different simulation exercises and scenarios to realize and reflect on the factors that shape their own decisions as well as moral values and beliefs. The course will utilize lectures, tutorials, case studies, and sharing of hands-on experience to guide students in adopting an all-rounded, holistic, and perspective-based approach to discuss and resolve the bioethical dilemmas related to health, life, and death matters.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for Biomedical Sciences; Public Health majors; and students who have taken PHPC3012. 
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.course_syllabus:
* *1.** **Bioethics and moral status: What makes living organisms possess moral status?** The basis of moral status, and why human beings are considered to possess moral status form the foundation of the study of bioethics. As human beings are agreed to possess full moral status, this introductory lecture will engage students in a reflection on what humans are by nature, and how human is viewed from the moral perspective, i.e. what are the specific human features that ground human moral status. This session will include cases to illustrate and explore the strengths and limitations of different ways for theories to define moral status.

* *2.** **Moral theories: Consequentialist utilitarianism** (the consequence of an action e.g. the number of lives that matters most morally) **vs. Categorical deontology** (the universal principle of the duty of the action itself that matters more morally) using classic trolleyology as example: what matters more? The strengths and limitations of the two main traditional moral theories. This session will apply deontology and utilitarianism to analyze a case involving white lies in healthcare settings.

* *3. Moral theories: Principlism.** Recognize the origin of the 4 pillars of principlism. Identify the bioethical issues and features of scenarios in the medical and healthcare settings. Learn the principlist method to resolve the moral dilemmas identified among the four principles in different scenarios. Recognize the deficiency and limitations of principlism in resolving moral dilemmas - why principlism necessary but inadequate in addressing the dilemma of life and death issues in the public health and healthcare settings. (e.g. Who to save? Decision-making under limited healthcare resources.Treat or not treat? Life-sustaining and standard curative treatments for end-of-life and terminally ill patients.)

* *4. Real life bioethical scenarios in Hong Kong and around the world.** Start a conversation: the pressing healthcare-related issues in Hong Kong (end-of-life care, organ donation, advanced therapies, Hong Kong Genome Project). Present some landmark and relevant local and international cases of bioethics in clinical and healthcare settings. Illustrate and analyze the moral dilemma and their respective bioethical arguments. Describe means of resolution of these conflicts.

* *5. Moral theories: Virtue ethics and character development.** From social psychology to bioethics: Milgrim Experiment. Does virtue exist in human beings or is it relative? Is pursuing virtue a meaningful project for human beings? Does character matter in bioethics? Describe the relevance of virtue and character to bioethics, power imbalance and coercion, bioethics in experiments and clinical trials (e.g. new drugs for a terminally ill patient), the importance of informed consent and the right to withdraw. This session will examine why one may commit a virtue-based wrong, such as using conscientious objection in healthcare as a case study.

* *6.** **Beyond reasons in bioethics: Emotions, intuitions, experience, and relationships.** Hume’s Sentimentalism as a moral theory (is moral decision governed by emotion or reason more?). How sentimentalism, intuition, experience and relationships influence decision-making in bioethics and healthcare settings. Who to save? Should relationship and feelings matter? Reviewing “The Imitation Game” scenario. Case discussion: organ transplant, resuscitation of terminally ill loved ones/family members, body donation.

* *7. Beyond traditional moral theories in bioethics: Non-Western, religion, care, narrative, and feminist ethics.** Non-Western ethics: a summary of how Confucianism or other non-Western ethics (e.g. African bioethics) approach bioethical issues. Medical care against cultural or religious beliefs (Jehovah witness, non-therapeutic infant male circumcision). Care ethics: Case-based perspective vs principle-based perspective. Narrative ethics: An alternative but necessary method to bring particularity and lived experience into bioethics. Feminist ethics: is ethics gendered? How does feminist perspective contribute to understanding the pluralism of bioethics? This session will analyze issues related to moral relativism through a case study, such as whether Médecins Sans Frontières should intervene or respect local customs like female genital cutting in Africa.

* *8. Living in a pluralistic world of bioethics: Perspectives vs foundational approach to bioethics.** Monism vs. Pluralism: why we need to approach bioethics in a pluralistic manner? The limitations of monistic approach to bioethics. Why and how we should approach bioethics using different perspectives and lenses. This session will apply a pluralistic approach to analyze the trade-offs of various pandemic control measures during COVID-19.

* *Tutorials:**

* *1.** **Decision-making in healthcare settings (with simulation exercise):** An introduction to how the principles of respect for autonomy, non-maleficence, beneficence and justice are relevant and featured in healthcare scenarios. Identify the moral dilemmas in the scenarios, and reflect on the rationale behind the decisions.

* *2. Moral decision-making of life and death matters (with simulation exercise to inform AI decision making):** Moral Machine Exercise of Automated Car. Identify and reflect on feelings, sentiments as well as reasoning that go into the decision-making process. Recognize personal values and what shape one’s moral decision regarding life and death matters.

* *3. Case study: Bringing it all together:** Use case study to illustrate how a pluralistic approach to bioethics works.

* *Glossary/Notes:**

* **Care ethics:** How about the genuine care and concerns for other people and living beings?
* **Feminist ethics:** Can we treat the moral theories available not as foundations but different perspectives?
* **Minority ethics:** What about the moral concerns of the minority?
* **Narrative ethics:** How about the stories and experiences behind people’s decisions?
* **Principlism:** Moral theories that are based on one single principle seems to be too narrow… how about one that has four?
* **Trolleyology:** stems from the “trolley problem” or “trolley dilemma” invented by the contemporary British philosopher Phillipa Foot in defending the doctrine of double effect (the morality of killing to save the life of others). Trolleyology is an evolved version of the trolley problem with scenarios that trigger different moral considerations behind judgment and a chosen action.
* **Virtue ethics:** Is it the act or the character of the person who does the act that counts morally?

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Freedom and Destiny
courses.description: Humans always hope for freedom. But we are finite beings. Most of what happens to us is beyond our control and decision. Is our will free? Is life determined? In what sense can we make a free choice? What can and what should we do in the face of destiny? This course will deal with these significant problems and the tension between freedom and destiny, in order to stimulate students’ reflection on their own lives.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for Philosophy Majors.
Co-requisite: UGFH1000, UGFN1000 or UGFN1001 (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Recognize and describe different views, traditional and contemporary, Chinese and Western, on “freedom” and “destiny”.

2. Analyze and elaborate the rationale behind those different views.

3. Compare and value those different views.

4. Reflect independently and critically on personal life views.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. 導論：本課主題與實存人生的相關性（教師可從現實社會的現象入手，如「占卜算命的流行及其反映的訊息」等，引導學員認識本課的意義）

2. 從純粹思辯的途徑探討自由與命運問題（核心內容）：

2. 1 命定論 (fatalism) 的論證

2. 2 決定論 (determinism) 的論證

2. 3 自由意志 (free will) 的論證

2. 4 思辯進路的功能與限制

3. 人面對命運時所常有的感觸（3.1 - 3.3為核心內容）：

3. 1 存在主義 (Existentialism) 論人生的荒謬

3. 2 佛教論無常

3. 3莊子的無奈感

3. 4海德格 (Heidegger) 論徬徨

4. 自由的探尋（一）：以心靈的灑脫超越無奈（4.1為核心內容）：

4. 1 莊子的「逍遙」

4. 2 佛教的「真實觀」與「解脫」

5. 自由的探尋（二）：以意志或氣質的強度克服命運（5.1為核心內容）：

5. 1 尼采 (Nietzsche)

5. 2叔本華 (Schopenhauer)

5. 3中國的英雄俠義（歷史與小說）

6. 自由的探尋（三）：以承擔為人的自由所在（6.4為核心內容）：

6. 1 希臘悲劇的承擔精神

6. 2基督教的信仰之路

6. 3康德 (Kant) 論人的歷史身份

6. 4儒家的道德實踐進路

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Humanity and Nature in Crisis: Values, Lifeways, and the Future
courses.description:
This course asks what we should value in nature (Units 1 and 2), how we should live with respect to it (Unit 3), and what future we should imagine for humanity and the planet (Unit 4). In Unit 1, we orient ourselves in our current situation: the Anthropocene, the planetary period in which a single species, human beings, has become the dominant force shaping the present and future of the planet. We also acquire some core theoretical tools for investigating what we value and how we should live. In Unit 2, we ask what, if anything, we should care about beyond our fellow human beings. Should we care about animals, plants, and ecosystems, and how should we act towards them? In Unit 3, we turn to various complementary disciplines across the humanities and social sciences to explore how humans might live differently within and towards the nonhuman natural world. In Unit 4, we turn to questions of the future, asking what kind of world we should imagine and seek to bring about for humanity and nonhuman life, on planet Earth and beyond. Where relevant, we will explore these topics through regional and local examples.

This course bears on several of the United Nations’ Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). We will see how work in the humanities and arts can motivate climate action (SDG#13 Climate Action). By reimagining our relationship with the environment and the connections between nature and culture, we can envision more sustainable ways of life in a heavily urbanized space like Hong Kong (SDG#11 Sustainable Cities and Communities). And by transforming our attitude towards and relationship with nature, we enjoy the mental health benefits of a deepened appreciation of and connection with nature (SDG#3 Good Health and Well-Being). With its special focus on environmental issues in Hong Kong, this course also addresses conservation concerns for Life Below Water (SDG#14) and Life On Land (SDG#15). The humanities and arts provide an alternative lens through which to understand today’s economic, social, and environmental problems, and the needs of those affected by climate change.

courses.course_syllabus:
1. Ethics in the Anthropocene
1. Living in the Anthropocene
2. Values and ethical theory
2. What should we care about?
1. Humans
2. Animals
3. Plants
4. Ecosystems, biodiversity, and species
3. How should we live?
1. Attention: Natural history
2. Imagination: Literature and arts
3. Remembering: Environmental history
4. Consuming: Materials, consumption, and waste
5. Making: Biophilic design
6. Legislating: Nature in the law
7. Healing: Ecopsychology
8. Growing: Mindfulness and nature
9. Seeing differently: Indigenous perspectives
4. Which future should we choose?
1. Growth, degrowth, and sustainability
2. Beyond human being
3. Beyond planet Earth
4. Population
5. Human extinction
6. Dying, naturally

All topics in Units 1 and 2 will be covered. Topics listed under Units 3 and 4 may vary across instructors

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Philosophy of Love
courses.description: This course aims at a philosophical discussion on the theories of love in the Chinese and Western culture. Topics include: evaluation of the philosophical approach to the phenomena of love and the Chinese and Western tradition, classical Western theories of love, Christian thought on love, traditional Chinese theories of love, and philosophy of love in modern society.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for Philosophy Majors; and students who have taken GDRS2003 or PHIL3373.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Recognize and understand different concepts and theories about love and their importance.
2. Critically analyze and evaluate the experiences of love and formulate their own understanding of love affair.
3. Recognize the relationship between love experience and the development of self-understanding, and the value of human life as a whole.
courses.course_syllabus:
(1-5) 核心內容。任教老師必須講授的專題
(6-10) 選項。任教老師可自行選擇講授的專題，以下所列僅供參考。

1. 導論：愛情之謎 The Puzzles of Love
2. 愛的古典理論I : 柏拉圖論*Eros*
3. 愛的古典理論II : 亞里士多德之德愛(*Philia*)
4. 愛的古典理論III : 基督教的博愛觀(*Agape*)
5. 愛的古典理論IV : 傳統中國思想中的「情」(*Qing*)

1. 愛的現代理論I：叔本華的性愛形上學
2. 愛的現代理論II：佛洛伊德的心理分析
3. 愛的現代理論III：鮑曼論液態愛情
4. 愛的現代理論IV：沙特的存在主義
5. 愛的現代理論V：西蒙．波娃的女性主義

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Philosophy of Travel
courses.description: This course examines the philosophical reflection on travel in both Chinese and Western culture. It will provide students with a good understanding of the multifaceted relationships between travel and human existence from an interdisciplinary perspective. What make us go? How the travel or tourist experiences have been constructed? Topics to be discussed include: different forms of travel throughout history; mobility and the dynamics of space and time; the death of distance and the end of travel; nomad philosophy and the meaning of travel in the post-modern world.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for Philosophy Majors.
Co-requisite: UGFH1000, UGFN1000 or UGFN1001 (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.course_syllabus:
* *1.****Introduction**

1. 1Defining “Travel” and “Tourism”

1. 2Chinese Traditional Concept of Travel

1. 3Western Tradition Concept of Travel

* *2.****Different Forms of Travel and Mobility Throughout History**

2. 1Travel as Expedition

2. 2Travel as Adventure

2. 3Travel as Pilgrimage

2. 4Travel as Missionary Quest

2. 5Travel as Exile

2. 6Travel as Escape

2. 7Travel as Self-Cultivation

2. 8Travel as Self-Discovery

2. 9Travel as Expression of Freedom

2. 10Travel as Transformation of Social Being

2. 11Travel as Consumerism

* *3.****Philosophical Issues in Travel and Tourism**

3. 1Motivation: Wonder, Exotics, Pleasure, Utopia, Beauty

3. 2Strangeness and Familiarity

3. 3Nature and Landscape

3. 4Leisure and Holiday

3. 5Home and At-homeness

* *4.****The Tourist Gaze and the Construction of Travel Experiences**

4. 1The Tourist Gazer and Gazee

4. 2The Subjective and Negotiated Characteristic of A Traveler’s Experience

4. 3The Sociological Dynamics of Guest-Host Encounter

* *5.****Mobility and the Dynamics of Space and Time**

5. 1Transportation Before the Railroad: Wayfarer and Carriage

5. 2The Industrialization of Time and Space in the 19th Century: Railroad Journey

5. 3Underground and Subway Vision

5. 4Aeroplane and Spaceship

6. **Travel in the Post/Transmodern World**

6. 1Mass Tourism: The New Colonialism

6. 2The Death of Distance, E-Tourism and the End of Travel

6. 3Nomadology and Nomad Philosophy

6. 4Human Existence and the Meaning of Travel

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Death and Immortality
courses.description: This course aims at an interdisciplinary study of the problem of death and immortality. The ideas of death and the arguments for and against the possibility of life after death in both Chinese and Western culture will be examined from the religious, psychological, and philosophical perspectives. Classical and modern theories on this topic will be discussed. The purpose of this course is to examine the meaning of life from a theoretical reflection on its apparent opposite—death, thus enhancing students' ability to think critically about life and death.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for Philosophy Majors; and students who have taken PHIL3383. Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Identify different aspects of the phenomena of death.
2. Relate to patients with chronic diseases, and be sympathetic to their needs and agony.
3. Reflect on the meaning of life in contrast to death.
courses.course_syllabus:
(1-5) 核心內容。任教老師必須講授的專題

(6-9) 選項。任教老師可自行選擇講授的專題，以下所列僅供參考。

1. 導論—死亡問題的性質

2. 不同文化的死亡觀

3. 處理死亡的各種進路：諸如醫學、心理學、社會學及藝術

4. 不朽的意義

5. 各種不朽的理論

6. 死亡的恐懼感

7. 各種死後的想像

8. 現代人的死亡觀

9. 科技與長生不死的構想

10. 文學、藝術中的死亡題材與意象

courses.required_readings:
Basic text:

By topics:

1. 導論—死亡問題的性質

閱讀材料：《令人著迷的生與死》第1章, 關子尹,『死亡的意義－論生死之間與幽明之際』

2. 不同文化的死亡觀

閱讀材料：What Death Means Now, chapter 1& 7, 《令人著迷的生與死》第6與第8章，《凝視死亡─死與人間的多元省思》第13章， Kubler-Ross Elisabeth, On death and dying, chapter 1-2

4. 不朽的意義

閱讀材料：《令人著迷的生與死》第11章, John Fischer, “Why immortality is not so bad”

5. 各種不朽的理論

閱讀材料 ：胡適，〈不朽：我的宗教〉, 唐君毅，〈死生之說與幽明之際〉, Bernard Williams, 'The Makropulos Case:Reflections on the Tedium of Immortality'

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Philosophy, Film and Life
courses.description:
The course aims to explore aspects of human life as reflected in philosophy and film. It introduces aesthetic concepts, film languages, and different schools and styles of film-making. It also explores the philosophical implications of films. Through selected films, students will be encouraged to develop critical thinking skills and concern for the meaning of life. Discussion will focus on three categories:
(1) films concerning humanities: the nobleness and darkness of human nature, the joy and sadness of love, and the absurdity of life; (2) films concerning virtual reality: the implications of the emulated world in sci-fi films, self-consciousness, and the crisis of false memory; (3) the metaphysical significance in films: the image of death, the origin of holiness and evil, and theism versus atheism.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for Philosophy Majors. 
Co-requisite: UGFH1000, UGFN1000 or UGFN1001 (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum).
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Describe and analyze some basic concepts and theories of philosophy.

2. Understand the basic concepts in film language and aesthetics.

3. Discuss the philosophical elements of some major genre of films.

4. Appreciate the contribution of philosophy to our understanding and appreciation of films.

5. Reflect philosophically on our experience in daily life through film appreciation.

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Exploring Sign Languages and Deaf Communities
courses.description: The course aims to introduce to students conceptions and misconceptions about sign language and deaf community. Crucially it explores how deaf and hearing people perceive sign language and deafness and how such perceptions from the hearing and deaf communities might lead to ideological conflicts in the preservation of socio-cultural values of the deaf community and its language, in the methodology and technology of speech and language rehabilitation, and in the mode of deaf education. The course will address these conflicting ideologies from the perspectives of linguistics, sociology, cultural studies, psychology, medicine and education.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for Linguistic Majors.
Co-requisite: UGFH1000, UGFN1000 or UGFN1001 (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.component:
Discussion
Lecture
Project
Interactive Tutorial
Workshop
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Modes of communication

* Modes of Communication in the Hearing world

+ Speech

+ Co-speech gestures

* Modes of Communication in the Deaf world

+ Gestures

+ Natural sign languages

+ Artificial signing systems

+ Cued speech

* Sign languages as a human instinct

+ Basic grammatical properties

+ The visual and spatial properties of sign languages

1. Exploring Deaf and Hearing Communities

* Sociolinguistics of a speech community

+ Disequilibrium between sign language and spoken language

+ Being Deaf or being Hearing-Impaired?

+ Audism or Deafhood?

+ Deaf or deaf?

+ Deaf identity & self esteem

1. The growth of language as reminiscence of humanity and culture

* Oral language development

+ Speech and language rehabilitation

+ Assistive hearing technology

+ Problems confronting parents and teachers of Deaf children

* Sign Bilingual development

+ Linguistic and cultural diversity

+ Biculturalism & Bilingualism

+ Code mixing and code blending in sign bilinguals

4. Educating Deaf students

* History of controversies

* Special education or Deaf education

* Deaf and Hearing teachers: do they make a difference

* Technology for supporting deaf students

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Reflections on Everyday Life
courses.description:
This course provides an opportunity for students to reflect on the formation of "self" in society and how the society affects our understanding of love, consumption, education, work, and meaning of life. Selected references in sociology, psychology, religion and literature will be introduced to provide a board intellectual perspective to understand the complex relationships between self and society. Students are expected to acquire an ability to analyze and critique on how different social institutions such as education, religion, family, media, work, consumerism, state, etc., affect our attitude and behaviors in some essential issues of life such as mate choice. Following the tradition of critical theory, this course assumes that this reflection is the first step for liberating from "bad faith" and living an authentic life.

Through reflection on experience that affects them most, students are encouraged to critically examine sustainability issues concerning the well-beings of young people (topic 1, 2, 3 and SDG3), quality of education (topic 4 and SDG4), sustainability in the workplace (topic 5 and SDG8) and inequalities in the local context (topic 3, 4, 5 and SDG10).
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for Sociology Majors; and students who have taken SOCI1202.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.course_attributes:
SDG-GE #3 Good Health and Well-being
SDG-GE #4 Quality Education
SDG-GE #8 Decent Work and Economic Growth
SDG-GE #10 Reducing Inequality
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Critical mind – to look some distance beyond the commonly accepted or officially defined goals of human actions.
2. Good observation – the ability to grasp the relationship between our individual lives and the larger social forces that help to shape them.
3. Ability to read books and apply knowledge.
4. To comprehend the interconnectedness of everyday life and selected sustainability issues, such as wellbeing, education, work and inequality (SDG3, 4, 8, 10).
courses.course_syllabus:
Fundamental Concepts

Social control, social norms, self, role, socialization, internalization, interpretation, definition of situation, world-taken-for-granted, ideology, bad faith, dependence, conformity, sense of security, freedom, autonomy, spontaneity.

Selected topics

1. Self and Society – discusses the “bad faith” and possibility of freedom in everyday life; and the “presentation of self”. Major concepts introduced: social control, social norms, self, role, socialization, internalization, interpretation, definition of situation, world-taken-for-granted, ideology, bad faith, dependence, conformity, sense of security, freedom, autonomy.

1. Love in a Commercialized World – discusses the rise of romantic love and the deceptive nature of erotic love, the making of the “modern traditional” family illustrated by the case of wedding; and how the commercialized world and the individualization process in post-industrial society further erode the “art of loving”. Major concepts introduced: self, role, dependence, sense of security, individualization, capitalism, modernity, post-modernity.

1. Digital Selves in the Online World – discusses the making and presentation of multiple selves since the digital revolution and consumer society; has the “one dimensional man” turned to be a genuine individual in the internet or becoming more monotonous and “one dimensional”? Major concepts: digital age, consumer society, freedom, autonomy, spontaneity, resistance, selves.

1. The Social Construction of Childhood and Modern Education – discusses the “discovery of childhood” and the emergence of the “priceless child” in the capitalist economy. Major concepts: socialization, parenting, value of education and learning.

1. Work and Corrosion of Character and Emotion – discusses how the “flexible regime” of work in new capitalism impacts on the formulation of character, the meaning of life, and the trust in community; and how deep-acting in service sector damages people’s emotional capacity. Major concepts introduced: self, role, socialization, internalization, character, emotional labor, decline of career.

1. Meaning of Life in Late-Modernity – discusses how different cultures and societies define the meaning of life. Major concepts introduced: self, role, socialization, internalization, spontaneity, self-actualization, making sense of life.

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Meanings of Life
courses.description:
(UGED2980 is double-coded with ANTH2350.)

In societies across the world, people have different culturally-shaped senses of what their lives mean: people may live for money, love, honour or fame; for family, company, country or religion. This course examines the processes through which meanings of life are formulated and maintained in day-to-day life in societies past and present; it also explores life meaning as a way of comprehending the evolution of human societies. The course uses the concept of "meanings of life" as a window into the anthropological understanding of cultural difference and cultural evolution.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGED2980 is double-coded with ANTH2350. 
Not for Anthropology Majors; and students who have taken ANTH2350.
Co-requisite: UGFH1000, UGFN1000 or UGFN1001 (only applicable to students admitted to the 4-year curriculum)
courses.learning_outcomes:
In this course, students learn about the processes through which individual meanings of life are formed and negotiated between individuals and their societies; they learn this through reading and analysis of individual life stories in different societies, and (optionally) through conducting and analyzing their own interviews. Students also learn about the evolution of senses of meaning across human history, from prehistory up until the present and future, in a range of different societies.

Taken together, these two areas of learning will enable students to think about human societies past and present, and about human lives lived within these societies, from a new perspective. In addition, it is expected that students will be able to think about their own lives and personal meanings in a deeper and broader and more conscious way; in this sense, although this course is academic, it transcends the purely academic, to have a potential impact on students' own thinking about how to

live their lives.

By the end of the course, students will be expected: a) to be able to think objectively, analytically and critically about meanings of life within their own and other societies, and understand the complex linkages between individuals and society in creating meaning, b) to be able to comprehend the broad panorama of the evolution of meaning over the course of human history, and c) (this objective, although discussed in class, is purposely left untouched by examinations) to be able to understand their own lives in a fuller and more insightful way.

(Note: Because this course is taught by only one person, all is core content)

Week Eight: Meanings of Life and Relativism: Do We Know More Than People in the Past?

Week Nine: Meanings of Life over Cultural Evolution: h m Hunter-Gatherers to Industrialists

Week Ten: The History of Meaning in Europe

Week Eleven: The History of Meaning in China and Japan

Week 'helve: Meanings of Life in Contemporary Societies: Buying, Selling, and Sanity

Week Thirteen: The Future of Meaning: All the World a Disneyland?

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Literature and Film
courses.description:
(UGED3143 is double-coded with ENGE3220.)

The aim of this course is to familiarize students with the kindred relationships between film and literature as well as their essential mediumistic differences such as film and the novel, film and drama/theatre, film and poetry, etc. Their similar or different uses of time and space, and problems of adaptation will be discussed in some works which have both filmic and literary versions.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGED3143 is double-coded with ENGE3220. Not for English; ELED Majors; and students who have taken ENGE3220. 
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.learning_outcomes:
It is hoped that after taking this course students will be able to:

Analyze film narratives and compare them to their literary counterparts.

Have a better understanding of how different film techniques contribute to creating meaning in visual media.

Apply their knowledge to the films they may watch outside the classroom as well as be aware of the literary sources from which films might be adaptated.

courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Love and Intimate Relationship
courses.description:
(UGED3208 is double-coded with GDRS3004.)

This course reviews basic concepts, theories and research findings of romantic love and intimate relationship. Various aspects of romantic love and intimate relationship will be critically reviewed. Based on research from a wide range of disciplines including gender studies, cultural studies, sociology, psychology, counselling, media studies, among various fields, together with input from literature and popular culture, the course will cover major areas such as the following: how do we understand the notions of “love” and “intimacy” in the different social and personal contexts, what are the different stages in a relationship; the influence of cultural myths and social scripts; interpersonal processes within romantic relationships (such as attraction, social cognition, communication and conflicts, etc.); how social gender role shapes romantic feelings and behaviors; notions of equality and freedom within relationships; power and violence; intimate relationship across life span, etc.

Students are encouraged to actively reflect upon their own life experience, to engage in critical discussions of cultural phenomenon through case studies, and learn to untangle the cultural myths, norms, and stereotypes that shapes our private lives and our society in general.

Upon the completion of the course, students should demonstrate basic understanding of structure, functions, dynamics, and development of romantic love and other interpersonal relationship. The personal is political. As personal relationships are the most basic social relationships that makes up the larger network of social relationships, it is hope that students could use knowledge from this course as an insight to understand, develop, and improve the quality of their own interpersonal relationship, and would aspire to build a more open and equal society starting from equalitarian and mindful personal relationships.

Through critical exercise and personal reflection on notions and experience of Love and Intimate Relationship in relations to notions such as power, diversity, equality, and freedom, the course engages directly with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) adopted by all United Nations Member States in 2015 by engaging directly with the SDGs 5 (Gender Equality), 10 (Reduced Inequalities) and 16 (Peace, Justice, and Strong Institution).
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGED3208 is double-coded with GDRS3004. 
Not for Gender Studies Majors; and students who have taken GDRS3004. 
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.course_attributes:
SDG-GE #5 Gender Equality
SDG-GE #10 Reducing Inequality
SDG-GE #16 Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions
courses.learning_outcomes:
Expected Learning Goals

1. Understand the knowledge base of romantic love and intimate relationship

2. Be equipped with the research methods in romantic love and intimate relationship from multiple disciplines such as psychology, cultural studies, sociology, philosophy and anthropology

3. Relate the knowledge on love and intimate relationships that they learn from the course to their everyday life

4. Develop their own values on love and intimate relationships after considering relevant theoretical views and empirical research

5. Gain insights about romantic love and intimate relationship

6. Critically reflect on gender and other inequalities in society and interpersonal relationships

Learning Outcomes

·Understand, relate and apply the key romantic love and intimate relationship concepts to daily life

·Critically engage with gender and other inequalities in society and interpersonal relationships

·Familiarize with the key classical and contemporary research findings in romantic love and intimate relationship

·Conceptualize, structure, articulate and present original ideas, both in written assignments and during tutorial discussions, on romantic love and intimate relationship related topics

·Design, direct and regulate own study plan for problem-based learning, from identifying the problem, setting learning objectives, researching on relevant information, reflecting on what has been learnt, to applying the knowledge to future problems
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1

* *What is Love? What is Intimacy?**

Topics

Week 3

SDG: 5 (Gender Equality), 10 (Reduced Inequalities), 16 (Peace, Justice, and Strong Institution).

Week 7

Week 8

SDG: 5 (Gender Equality), 10 (Reduced Inequalities), 16 (Peace, Justice, and Strong Institution).

Week 9

Topics:

SDG: 5 (Gender Equality), 10 (Reduced Inequalities), 16 (Peace, Justice, and Strong Institution).

Week 10

* *Recognising Trauma**

Topics:

SDG: 5 (Gender Equality), 10 (Reduced Inequalities), 16 (Peace, Justice, and Strong Institution).

Week 11

* *Breaking up, leaving, and the way forward**

Topics:

SDG: 5 (Gender Equality), 10 (Reduced Inequalities)

SDG: 5 (Gender Equality), 10 (Reduced Inequalities), 16 (Peace, Justice, and Strong Institution).
