courses.subject: UGED
courses.title: Buddhism and Contemporary Life
courses.description:
(UGED3322 is double-coded with CURE3145.)

This course studies Buddhism and contemporary life in the context of the new development of Buddhism. It examines how Buddhism is reinvented and reconceptualized to make it meaningful for men and women living in modern complex society of the information age. Based on the recent development of Buddhism in both Asia and in the West, this course discusses how Buddhism responds to contemporary issues facing contemporary men and women. Topics include the Buddhist approach to economic development and human right, pollution and biotechnology, feminism, Buddhist ethics and the issues of abortion, euthanasia and suicide, Buddhism and interfaith dialogue.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
UGED3322 is double-coded with CURE3145.Not for Cultural Studies; Public Humanities; Religious Studies; Theology Majors; and students who have taken CURE3145 or UGED2331.
Co-requisite: Any course with “UGFH” or “UGFN” as prefix
courses.learning_outcomes:
uTo encourage students to probe deeper into the ground of existence and develop a wider context of meaning of life through a critical study of the development of Buddhism in modern society.

uTo help student understand how religion can exert positive influences on human life and modern society through the case of Buddhism
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Week One:** Introduction to Buddhist Studies: The Life of Buddha (CD)

* *Week Two**: The Buddha and his religion m: Brief History and Major Doctrine

(Highlight: the life of the Buddha, doctrines of causality-Cause and Effect, non-self, impermanence, and suffering)

Donald S. Lopez, Jr. “Introduction” in Donald S. Lopez, Jr, ed., Buddhism in Practice, ( Princeton University Press, 1995: 3-36) (Optional)

* *Week Two:** Suffering and Buddhist Solution

(Highlight: (Are human beings happy? What are the causes for human suffering and inequality? What is the solution?)

Walpola Rahula, *What the Buddha Taught* (Oxford: Oneworld, 1997)

* *Week Three**: Buddhist World: Mahayana Buddhism and Theravada Buddhism:

(Buddhist traditions and history in general: Mahayana in East Asia and Theravada in South-East Asia, different and common ideas and practices between the two

Carl Olson, *The Different Path of Buddhism: A narrative-Historical Introduction*, (New York: Rutgers University Press, 2005)

* *Week Tour:** Engaged Buddhism in Modern World:

(Highlight: social impact on Buddhism in Asia, Buddhist revival and national awakening in the early 20th century, expansion of Buddhism in the West)

\*Don Pittman, *Toward A modern Chinese Buddhism: Taixu’s Reforms*. Honolulu: University of Havai’i Press, 2001: 13-60.

Christopher Queen, “Introduction: The Shapes and Sources of Engaged Buddhism” in ibid*.*, *Engaged Buddhism: Buddhist Liberation Movements in Asia*. New (York: State University of New York Press, 1996):1-44.

* *Week Five:** Humanistic Buddhism: Contemporary Social Practices of Buddhism

(Highlight: What are new for modern Buddhism? How have Buddhists recreated their Buddhist traditions in contemporary world?)

C. Julia Huang, “The Buddhist Tzu-Chi Foundation of Taiwan” in Christopher Queen, ed., *Action Dharma: New Studies in Engaged Buddhism*. (Routledge: Curzon, 2003):134-151.

Stuart Chandler, *Establishing A Pure Land on Earth: The Foguang Buddhist Perspective on Modernization and Globalization*. Honolulu: University of Havai’i Press, 2004: 43-77.

* *Week Six:** Buddhism and New Religion (Tour to A Buddhist Site in HK)

(Highlight: Have modern Buddhists betrayed Buddhism?

George Chryssides, *Exploring New Religions*. (London and New York: CASSELL, 1999): 204-243.

* *Week Seven:** Buddhism and Rebirth

(Highlight: Why we are not equal? Where did human beings come from? Is death the end of human life?)

Ian Stevenson, “Three Cases Suggestive of Reincarnation in Ceylon,” in ibid, ed., *Twenty Cases Suggestive of Reincarnation*. (Charlottesville: University Press of Virginia, 1995, 4th printing): 128-180.

* *Week Eight:** Meditation and Business Management

(Highlight: Are we happier now? How can we settle down our mind which is full of burdens? How can we be a successful business leader?)

Sheng-Yen, “Zen Meditation” in *Zen Tradition and Transition*, Kenneth Kraft, ed. (New York: Grove Press, 1988): 30-43.

Thich Thein-An, “Everyday is a Good Day” in *Zen Philosophy and Zen Practice*, (Dharma Pablishing, 1975):113-124

Jayatilal Shah, *Vipassana and Business Management* *http://www.buddhismtoday.com/english/manage/001-business.htm*

\*Eshin, *The Ten Oxherding Pictures*

http://www.buddhismtoday.com/english/meditation/Zen/002-tenpicture.htm

* *Week Nine:** Buddhism and Education

(Highlight: What is meant by education? What is the Buddhist way of teaching and learning? Can we learn what we want?)

Nathana Katz and Judith Simmer, “Buddhist Perspectives on Education,” in A. Adikari, ed., *Sambhasha*, (The Ministry of Education and Higher Education, 1991): 331-36. (Handouts)

* *Week Ten:** Feminism and Human Rights in Buddhism

(Highlight: What is the Buddhist attitude towards woman? What are human rights meant in Buddhism?)

S. Ichimura and B. P. Kirthisinghe, “Human Rights and the Buddhist Concept of Law and Norm,” in A Adikari, ed., *Sambhasha*. (A Publication of the Branch of Pirivena Education of the Ministry of Education and Higher Education, 1991): 338-354. (Handout)

\*John Powers, “Human Rights and Cultural Values: The Political Values of the Dalai Lama and the People’s Republic China,” in Damien Keown, ed., *Buddhism and Human Rights*. (Richmond: Curzon, 1998): 175-202.

Masatoshi Ueki, *Gender Equality in Buddhism.* (New York: Peter Lang, 2001): 3-32.

* *Week Eleven:** Buddhist Perspectives on Violence: Abortion and Euthanasia

(Highlight: What are Buddhist attitudes towards killing in general? Should one support abortion and euthanasia?)

Robert Florida, “Buddhism and Abortion,” in Damien Keown, ed., *Contemporary Buddhist Ethics*. (Richmond: Curzon, 2000): 137-168.

Pinit Ratanakul, “To Save or Let Go: Thai Buddhist Perspectives on Euthanasis,” in Damien Keown, ed., *Contemporary Buddhist Ethics*. (Richmond: Curzon, 2000): 169-182.

* *Week Twelve:** Buddhism and Ecology

(Highlight: What does Buddhism teach human beings in dealing with nature and other living beings? What could Buddhism propose to solve the environmental problems?)

Ian Harris, “Buddhism and Ecology,” in Damien Keown, ed. *Contemporary Buddhist Ethics*. (Richmond: Curzon 2000): 113-136.

Kenneth Kraft, “Nuclear Ecology and Engaged Buddhism,” in Marry Evelyn Tucker, ed., *Buddhism and Ecology: The Interconnection of Dharma and Deeds*. (Harvard University Press, 1997): 269-290.

David Landis Barnhill**, “**Relational Holism: Huayan Buddhism and Deep Ecology.” **David Landis Barnhill and Roger S. Gottlieb, eds., *Deep ecology and world religions: new essays on sacred grounds*.** Albany : State University of New York Press, c2001: 77-106.

* *Week Thirteen:** Buddhism and Science (Physical)

(Highlight: Can Buddhism go along with science? Should Buddhism (religion in general) make use of science to support its philosophy and practice?)

William Ames, “Emptiness and Quantum Theory,” in B. Alan Wallace, ed., *Buddhism and Science* (New York: Columbia University Press, 2003): 285-304.

Victor Mansfield, “Time and Impermanence in Middle Way Buddhism and Modern Physics,” in B. Alan Wallace, ed., *Buddhism and Science* (New York: Columbia University Press, 2003):305-324.

* *Week Fourteen:** Buddhism and Psychology

(Highlight: What is the relationship between the matter and mind in Buddhism? Can Buddhist analysis of the mind help our understanding of human life and its meaning?)

David Galin, “The Concepts “Self,” “Person,” and “I” in Western Psychology and in Buddhism,” in B. Alan Wallace, ed., *Buddhism and Science* (New York: Columbia University Press, 2003):107-144.

Gay Watson, “A Buddhist Inspiration for a Contemporary Psychotherapy,” in Tadeusz Skorupski, ed., *The Buddhist Forum: Volume IV*. (London: The University of London, 1996): 245-273

Notes:

Readings marked with \* are recommended for reading yet not compulsory.

Reading materials will be reserved in University Library, and some copies of them may be provided in advance.
